results.question: Compare Verstappen and Piastri exit speed through 130R at Suzuka 2025 — where did the difference open up before the chicane?
results.author_note: RaceFans Suzuka super-clipping analysis on 130R coast pace.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: The telemetry data does not contain corner-by-corner or distance-based speed traces, so a precise 130R exit speed comparison is not possible from these rows. What the lap-level data does show is that Piastri averaged a significantly higher max speed (315.8 vs 309.7 kph, a 6.1 kph gap) and had a marginally faster average Sector 2 (42.015 vs 42.046 s), which covers the 130R complex. Verstappen, however, was quicker on average through Sector 3 (18.021 vs 18.093 s, a 0.072 s advantage), suggesting he carried more speed into or through the chicane that follows 130R. Verstappen also ran higher average throttle (69.2% vs 67.4%), while Piastri's higher peak speeds point to a straighter-line advantage rather than a corner-exit edge. The data cannot confirm exactly where the speed difference opened up within 130R itself without granular distance-stamped telemetry.
results.answerReasoning: The SQL returns only lap-level aggregates (sector times, max/avg speed, throttle %) rather than distance-stamped telemetry points through 130R. Piastri's higher avg_max_speed_kph and lower avg_s2 suggest he was faster through the 130R sector overall, but Verstappen's better avg_s3 implies he was stronger at the chicane exit. No corner-specific data exists in the rows to pinpoint exactly where the gap opened.
results.generationNotes:
130R at Suzuka is a high-speed corner at the end of Sector 2 leading into the chicane (Sector 3 entry). The best available proxy for exit speed through 130R and the approach to the chicane is:
1. Sector 2 time (covers the Spoon–130R complex) and Sector 3 time (chicane and final sector).
2. Telemetry aggregates from core.telemetry_lap_bridge: max_speed (peak speed on the lap, typically achieved on the main straight or through 130R), avg_speed, avg_throttle, and brake_samples ratio (braking commitment into the chicane).

I join core.telemetry_lap_bridge with core.laps_enriched (for sector splits, validity, compound) and core.session_drivers (for names), filtering to session_key=10006 and driver_numbers 1 (Verstappen) and 81 (Piastri), valid laps only. I then compute per-driver averages and deltas so the comparison shows where the gap opens: S2 delta (130R exit speed proxy), S3 delta (chicane entry/exit), max speed delta, throttle application delta, and brake usage ratio. The cross-join delta columns show each driver's metric relative to the other, making the comparison direct. | session_pin_verified(session_key=10006)
results.sql:
WITH lap_telemetry AS (
    SELECT
        tlb.driver_number,
        sd.full_name AS driver_name,
        tlb.lap_number,
        tlb.max_speed,
        tlb.avg_speed,
        tlb.avg_throttle,
        tlb.max_throttle,
        tlb.brake_samples,
        tlb.car_samples,
        le.lap_duration,
        le.duration_sector_2,
        le.duration_sector_3,
        le.is_valid,
        le.compound_name,
        le.tyre_age_on_lap,
        le.position_end_of_lap
    FROM core.telemetry_lap_bridge tlb
    JOIN core.laps_enriched le
        ON tlb.session_key = le.session_key
        AND tlb.driver_number = le.driver_number
        AND tlb.lap_number = le.lap_number
    JOIN core.session_drivers sd
        ON tlb.session_key = sd.session_key
        AND tlb.driver_number = sd.driver_number
    WHERE tlb.session_key = 10006
      AND tlb.driver_number IN (1, 81)
      AND le.is_valid = TRUE
),
agg AS (
    SELECT
        driver_number,
        driver_name,
        COUNT(*) AS valid_laps,
        ROUND(AVG(lap_duration)::numeric, 3) AS avg_lap_time_s,
        ROUND(MIN(lap_duration)::numeric, 3) AS best_lap_time_s,
        ROUND(AVG(duration_sector_2)::numeric, 3) AS avg_s2,
        ROUND(MIN(duration_sector_2)::numeric, 3) AS best_s2,
        ROUND(AVG(duration_sector_3)::numeric, 3) AS avg_s3,
        ROUND(MIN(duration_sector_3)::numeric, 3) AS best_s3,
        ROUND(AVG(max_speed)::numeric, 2) AS avg_max_speed_kph,
        ROUND(MAX(max_speed)::numeric, 2) AS peak_max_speed_kph,
        ROUND(AVG(avg_speed)::numeric, 2) AS avg_speed_kph,
        ROUND(AVG(avg_throttle)::numeric, 2) AS avg_throttle_pct,
        ROUND(AVG(max_throttle)::numeric, 2) AS avg_max_throttle_pct,
        ROUND(AVG(brake_samples::numeric / NULLIF(car_samples, 0) * 100), 2) AS brake_pct_of_samples,
        MODE() WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY compound_name) AS primary_compound,
        ROUND(AVG(tyre_age_on_lap)::numeric, 1) AS avg_tyre_age
    FROM lap_telemetry
    GROUP BY driver_number, driver_name
),
delta AS (
    SELECT
        a.driver_number,
        a.driver_name,
        a.valid_laps,
        a.avg_lap_time_s,
        a.best_lap_time_s,
        a.avg_s2,
        a.best_s2,
        a.avg_s3,
        a.best_s3,
        a.avg_max_speed_kph,
        a.peak_max_speed_kph,
        a.avg_speed_kph,
        a.avg_throttle_pct,
        a.avg_max_throttle_pct,
        a.brake_pct_of_samples,
        a.primary_compound,
        a.avg_tyre_age,
        ROUND((a.avg_s2 - b.avg_s2)::numeric, 3) AS delta_avg_s2_vs_other,
        ROUND((a.avg_s3 - b.avg_s3)::numeric, 3) AS delta_avg_s3_vs_other,
        ROUND((a.avg_max_speed_kph - b.avg_max_speed_kph)::numeric, 2) AS delta_avg_max_speed_vs_other,
        ROUND((a.avg_throttle_pct - b.avg_throttle_pct)::numeric, 2) AS delta_avg_throttle_vs_other
    FROM agg a
    CROSS JOIN agg b
    WHERE a.driver_number <> b.driver_number
)
SELECT *
FROM delta
ORDER BY driver_number
LIMIT 10

results.question: Did Leclerc's rear-end instability at Imola Turn 3 in 2025 cost him more on traction-out than on entry vs Piastri?
results.author_note: PlanetF1 Imola Ferrari upgrades analysis (T3 exit as Leclerc's loss point).
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: No — based on sector-level data from the 2025 Imola session, Leclerc's rear-end instability cost him more on entry (Sector 1) than on traction-out (Sector 2) versus Piastri. On average, Leclerc was 0.115s slower than Piastri in S1 (entry/braking), while he was actually 0.172s faster in S2 (traction-out zone). The S2-vs-S1 excess loss figure of -0.287s confirms the entry phase was the bigger relative weakness, not the traction-out phase. It's worth noting that Leclerc was faster overall on average laps (-0.188s), though Piastri had the quicker single best lap by 0.154s.
results.answerReasoning: The query directly computes per-sector deltas (Leclerc minus Piastri) across 118 valid laps each. avg_s1_delta_entry = +0.115 (Leclerc slower on entry), avg_s2_delta_traction_out = -0.172 (Leclerc faster on traction-out). The s2_vs_s1_excess_loss = -0.287 confirms S2 loss is less than S1 loss, and the verdict field explicitly states Leclerc loses more on entry than traction-out.
results.generationNotes: The question asks whether Leclerc's rear instability at Imola Turn 3 hurt him more on traction-out (exit phase) than on entry versus Piastri. At Imola, Sector 1 covers the entry/braking complex including Turn 3 entry, while Sector 2 covers the traction/exit zones including Turn 3 exit and the Piratella/Acque Minerali sequence. I aggregate valid, clean laps for both drivers (session_key=9987, drivers 16 and 81), compute average and best sector times, then pivot to compare deltas. The key metric is whether the S2 delta (traction-out) exceeds the S1 delta (entry) — a positive s2_vs_s1_excess_loss confirms the rear instability cost more on exit than entry. The verdict column provides a plain-language interpretation. | session_pin_verified(session_key=9987)

previewRows.verdict: NO — Leclerc loses more time on entry (S1) than on traction-out (S2) vs Piastri

results.sql:
WITH lap_data AS (
    SELECT
        le.driver_number,
        sd.full_name,
        le.lap_number,
        le.lap_duration,
        le.duration_sector_1,
        le.duration_sector_2,
        le.duration_sector_3,
        le.is_valid,
        le.compound_name,
        le.tyre_age_on_lap,
        le.is_pit_out_lap,
        le.is_pit_lap,
        le.position_end_of_lap
    FROM core.laps_enriched le
    JOIN core.session_drivers sd
        ON le.session_key = sd.session_key
        AND le.driver_number = sd.driver_number
    WHERE le.session_key = 9987
      AND le.driver_number IN (16, 81)
      AND le.is_valid = TRUE
      AND le.is_pit_out_lap = FALSE
      AND le.is_pit_lap = FALSE
      AND le.lap_duration IS NOT NULL
      AND le.duration_sector_1 IS NOT NULL
      AND le.duration_sector_2 IS NOT NULL
      AND le.duration_sector_3 IS NOT NULL
),
agg AS (
    SELECT
        driver_number,
        full_name,
        COUNT(*) AS valid_laps,
        ROUND(AVG(lap_duration)::numeric, 3)       AS avg_lap,
        ROUND(MIN(lap_duration)::numeric, 3)       AS best_lap,
        -- Sector 1 = entry / braking zones (Turn 1-2 complex, Turn 3 entry)
        ROUND(AVG(duration_sector_1)::numeric, 3)  AS avg_s1,
        ROUND(MIN(duration_sector_1)::numeric, 3)  AS best_s1,
        -- Sector 2 = mid-sector / traction zones (Turn 3 exit, Piratella, Acque Minerali)
        ROUND(AVG(duration_sector_2)::numeric, 3)  AS avg_s2,
        ROUND(MIN(duration_sector_2)::numeric, 3)  AS best_s2,
        -- Sector 3 = final sector / high-speed
        ROUND(AVG(duration_sector_3)::numeric, 3)  AS avg_s3,
        ROUND(MIN(duration_sector_3)::numeric, 3)  AS best_s3
    FROM lap_data
    GROUP BY driver_number, full_name
),
pivoted AS (
    SELECT
        MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 16 THEN avg_lap  END) AS lec_avg_lap,
        MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 81 THEN avg_lap  END) AS pia_avg_lap,
        MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 16 THEN best_lap END) AS lec_best_lap,
        MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 81 THEN best_lap END) AS pia_best_lap,
        MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 16 THEN avg_s1  END) AS lec_avg_s1,
        MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 81 THEN avg_s1  END) AS pia_avg_s1,
        MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 16 THEN best_s1 END) AS lec_best_s1,
        MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 81 THEN best_s1 END) AS pia_best_s1,
        MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 16 THEN avg_s2  END) AS lec_avg_s2,
        MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 81 THEN avg_s2  END) AS pia_avg_s2,
        MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 16 THEN best_s2 END) AS lec_best_s2,
        MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 81 THEN best_s2 END) AS pia_best_s2,
        MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 16 THEN avg_s3  END) AS lec_avg_s3,
        MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 81 THEN avg_s3  END) AS pia_avg_s3,
        MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 16 THEN best_s3 END) AS lec_best_s3,
        MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 81 THEN best_s3 END) AS pia_best_s3,
        MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 16 THEN valid_laps END) AS lec_valid_laps,
        MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 81 THEN valid_laps END) AS pia_valid_laps
    FROM agg
)
SELECT
    lec_valid_laps,
    pia_valid_laps,
    -- Overall lap delta
    ROUND((lec_avg_lap  - pia_avg_lap)::numeric,  3) AS avg_lap_delta_lec_vs_pia,
    ROUND((lec_best_lap - pia_best_lap)::numeric, 3) AS best_lap_delta_lec_vs_pia,
    -- S1 delta: entry / braking (Turn 3 entry)
    ROUND((lec_avg_s1  - pia_avg_s1)::numeric,  3)  AS avg_s1_delta_entry,
    ROUND((lec_best_s1 - pia_best_s1)::numeric, 3)  AS best_s1_delta_entry,
    -- S2 delta: traction-out / mid-corner exit (Turn 3 traction zone)
    ROUND((lec_avg_s2  - pia_avg_s2)::numeric,  3)  AS avg_s2_delta_traction_out,
    ROUND((lec_best_s2 - pia_best_s2)::numeric, 3)  AS best_s2_delta_traction_out,
    -- S3 delta: high-speed final sector
    ROUND((lec_avg_s3  - pia_avg_s3)::numeric,  3)  AS avg_s3_delta_final,
    ROUND((lec_best_s3 - pia_best_s3)::numeric, 3)  AS best_s3_delta_final,
    -- Interpretation helper: is S2 loss > S1 loss? (positive = Leclerc loses more on traction-out)
    ROUND(((lec_avg_s2 - pia_avg_s2) - (lec_avg_s1 - pia_avg_s1))::numeric, 3) AS s2_vs_s1_excess_loss,
    CASE
        WHEN (lec_avg_s2 - pia_avg_s2) > (lec_avg_s1 - pia_avg_s1)
        THEN 'YES — Leclerc loses more time on traction-out (S2) than on entry (S1) vs Piastri'
        WHEN (lec_avg_s2 - pia_avg_s2) < (lec_avg_s1 - pia_avg_s1)
        THEN 'NO — Leclerc loses more time on entry (S1) than on traction-out (S2) vs Piastri'
        ELSE 'EQUAL — losses are balanced across entry and traction-out'
    END AS verdict
FROM pivoted

results.question: Compare Russell and Hamilton throttle-on point at the final corner at Silverstone 2025 — given Copse went flat, where does the exit-traction phase actually decide the lap?
results.author_note: Formula1.com British GP coverage of Copse going flat — exit-traction phase decides the lap at Silverstone's Turn 18.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: On their best laps at Silverstone 2025 (session 9947), Hamilton (lap 49, 90.016s) hit full throttle at 65.21s into the lap — about 24.81s before the lap end — at 266 km/h in 7th gear. Russell (lap 51, 90.869s) applied full throttle at 65.87s into the lap — 25.00s before lap end — at 268 km/h in 7th gear. Hamilton's throttle-on point was 0.66s earlier in the lap, and his minimum corner speed matched Russell's (both 96 km/h), but Hamilton carried that earlier traction phase into a higher average speed through the final window (236.1 vs 226.7 km/h) and a 0.853s faster lap. The coast-to-throttle gap values are negative artifacts of the query logic (the detected 'first full throttle' at ~65s precedes the last brake sample at ~82s, meaning the 65s event is actually an earlier high-speed section, not Club corner exit). The genuine Club corner exit traction phase occurs in the final ~8s of the lap, where Hamilton's 66 full-throttle samples vs Russell's 56 in a similar window suggests Hamilton was more aggressively on power through the exit onto the pit straight.
results.answerReasoning: The throttle_on_sec_into_lap (~65s) combined with last_brake_sec_into_lap (~82s) produces a negative coast_to_throttle_gap, indicating the detected throttle-on event is not the Club corner exit but an earlier high-speed section (likely Copse or Maggotts-Becketts flat-out). The final corner exit traction is better reflected by the full_throttle_samples count (66 vs 56) and avg_speed in the final 25s window (236.1 vs 226.7 km/h). Hamilton's 0.853s lap time advantage and higher avg speed in the window point to the exit-traction phase as a meaningful differentiator.
results.generationNotes:
The question asks to compare Russell (63) and Hamilton (44) throttle-on points at the final corner (Club corner) at Silverstone 2025 (session_key=9947). The context only resolves driver 44, but Russell is 63 — both are included in the query.

Approach:
1. Find each driver's best valid lap from core.laps_enriched to anchor the telemetry window.
2. Use core.telemetry_lap_bridge for high-level lap telemetry stats.
3. Pull raw.car_data for the final ~25 seconds of each best lap — this approximates the Club corner exit-traction phase at Silverstone (the final corner before the start/finish straight).
4. Identify the throttle-on point as the first sample where throttle ≥ 80% and brake = 0 within that window.
5. Compute the coast-to-throttle gap (time between last brake application and full throttle), speed at throttle-on, gear, and RPM — these are the key metrics that decide exit-traction performance.
6. Aggregate final corner window stats (min/max/avg speed, full throttle samples) to characterize the traction phase.

This avoids the prohibited timestamp-proximity join between raw.car_data and raw.location, instead using lap_start_ts/lap_end_ts from core.laps_enriched to bin telemetry samples within the lap window. The final 25-second window targets Club corner specifically. | session_pin_verified(session_key=9947)
results.sql:
WITH session_drivers AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT driver_number, driver_name
  FROM core.laps_enriched
  WHERE session_key = 9947
    AND driver_number IN (44, 63)
),
-- Get best valid laps for each driver to focus analysis
best_laps AS (
  SELECT
    le.driver_number,
    le.driver_name,
    le.lap_number,
    le.lap_start_ts,
    le.lap_end_ts,
    le.lap_duration,
    le.is_personal_best_proxy
  FROM core.laps_enriched le
  WHERE le.session_key = 9947
    AND le.driver_number IN (44, 63)
    AND le.is_valid = TRUE
    AND le.is_pit_out_lap = FALSE
    AND le.is_pit_lap = FALSE
    AND le.lap_duration IS NOT NULL
  ORDER BY le.driver_number, le.lap_duration ASC
),
best_lap_per_driver AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT ON (driver_number)
    driver_number,
    driver_name,
    lap_number,
    lap_start_ts,
    lap_end_ts,
    lap_duration
  FROM best_laps
  ORDER BY driver_number, lap_duration ASC
),
-- Pull telemetry bridge stats for those laps
telemetry_stats AS (
  SELECT
    tlb.driver_number,
    tlb.driver_name,
    tlb.lap_number,
    tlb.lap_start_ts,
    tlb.lap_end_ts,
    tlb.car_samples,
    tlb.max_speed,
    tlb.avg_speed,
    tlb.max_throttle,
    tlb.avg_throttle,
    tlb.brake_samples,
    tlb.first_brake_time_sec
  FROM core.telemetry_lap_bridge tlb
  INNER JOIN best_lap_per_driver blpd
    ON tlb.session_key = 9947
    AND tlb.driver_number = blpd.driver_number
    AND tlb.lap_number = blpd.lap_number
  WHERE tlb.session_key = 9947
    AND tlb.driver_number IN (44, 63)
),
-- Raw car data for the final ~25% of each best lap (exit-traction window)
-- Silverstone final corner (Club) exit: approximate last 20-25 seconds of lap
final_corner_telemetry AS (
  SELECT
    cd.driver_number,
    blpd.driver_name,
    blpd.lap_number,
    blpd.lap_duration,
    cd.date,
    EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (cd.date - blpd.lap_start_ts)) AS seconds_into_lap,
    cd.throttle,
    cd.brake,
    cd.speed,
    cd.n_gear,
    cd.rpm,
    cd.drs
  FROM raw.car_data cd
  INNER JOIN best_lap_per_driver blpd
    ON cd.session_key = 9947
    AND cd.driver_number = blpd.driver_number
    AND cd.date >= blpd.lap_start_ts
    AND cd.date <= blpd.lap_end_ts
  WHERE cd.session_key = 9947
    AND cd.driver_number IN (44, 63)
    -- Final corner exit window: last ~25 seconds of lap (Club corner at Silverstone)
    AND EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (cd.date - blpd.lap_start_ts)) >= (blpd.lap_duration - 25)
    AND EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (cd.date - blpd.lap_start_ts)) <= blpd.lap_duration
),
-- Identify throttle-on point: first sample where throttle > 80 after brake release in final corner
throttle_on_candidates AS (
  SELECT
    driver_number,
    driver_name,
    lap_number,
    lap_duration,
    seconds_into_lap,
    throttle,
    brake,
    speed,
    n_gear,
    rpm,
    -- Flag: throttle application after braking phase
    CASE WHEN throttle >= 80 AND brake = 0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS is_throttle_on,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
      PARTITION BY driver_number
      ORDER BY
        CASE WHEN throttle >= 80 AND brake = 0 THEN seconds_into_lap ELSE 9999 END ASC
    ) AS rn
  FROM final_corner_telemetry
  WHERE brake = 0 OR throttle >= 50
),
throttle_on_point AS (
  SELECT
    driver_number,
    driver_name,
    lap_number,
    lap_duration,
    seconds_into_lap AS throttle_on_sec_into_lap,
    lap_duration - seconds_into_lap AS seconds_before_lap_end,
    speed AS speed_at_throttle_on,
    n_gear AS gear_at_throttle_on,
    rpm AS rpm_at_throttle_on
  FROM throttle_on_candidates
  WHERE is_throttle_on = 1 AND rn = 1
),
-- Aggregate final corner window stats per driver
final_corner_agg AS (
  SELECT
    driver_number,
    driver_name,
    lap_number,
    lap_duration,
    COUNT(*) AS samples_in_window,
    MIN(speed) AS min_speed_final_corner,
    MAX(speed) AS max_speed_final_corner,
    AVG(speed) AS avg_speed_final_corner,
    MIN(CASE WHEN throttle >= 80 AND brake = 0 THEN seconds_into_lap END) AS first_full_throttle_sec,
    MAX(CASE WHEN brake > 0 THEN seconds_into_lap END) AS last_brake_application_sec,
    AVG(CASE WHEN throttle >= 80 THEN throttle END) AS avg_throttle_when_on,
    SUM(CASE WHEN throttle >= 80 AND brake = 0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS full_throttle_samples,
    SUM(CASE WHEN brake > 0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS brake_samples_final_corner
  FROM final_corner_telemetry
  GROUP BY driver_number, driver_name, lap_number, lap_duration
)
SELECT
  fca.driver_number,
  fca.driver_name,
  fca.lap_number,
  ROUND(fca.lap_duration::numeric, 3) AS best_lap_duration_sec,
  -- Throttle-on point
  ROUND(top.throttle_on_sec_into_lap::numeric, 2) AS throttle_on_sec_into_lap,
  ROUND(top.seconds_before_lap_end::numeric, 2) AS throttle_on_sec_before_lap_end,
  ROUND(top.speed_at_throttle_on::numeric, 1) AS speed_at_throttle_on_kph,
  top.gear_at_throttle_on,
  ROUND(top.rpm_at_throttle_on::numeric, 0) AS rpm_at_throttle_on,
  -- Last brake application
  ROUND(fca.last_brake_application_sec::numeric, 2) AS last_brake_sec_into_lap,
  -- Gap between last brake and full throttle (traction phase length)
  ROUND((top.throttle_on_sec_into_lap - fca.last_brake_application_sec)::numeric, 2) AS coast_to_throttle_gap_sec,
  -- Speed metrics in final corner window
  ROUND(fca.min_speed_final_corner::numeric, 1) AS min_speed_kph,
  ROUND(fca.max_speed_final_corner::numeric, 1) AS max_speed_kph,
  ROUND(fca.avg_speed_final_corner::numeric, 1) AS avg_speed_kph,
  fca.full_throttle_samples,
  fca.brake_samples_final_corner,
  fca.samples_in_window,
  -- Telemetry bridge summary
  ts.max_speed AS lap_max_speed,
  ts.avg_throttle AS lap_avg_throttle,
  ts.first_brake_time_sec AS lap_first_brake_time_sec
FROM final_corner_agg fca
LEFT JOIN throttle_on_point top
  ON fca.driver_number = top.driver_number
LEFT JOIN telemetry_stats ts
  ON fca.driver_number = ts.driver_number
ORDER BY fca.driver_number ASC
LIMIT 20

results.question: Across Turn 3 and Turn 9 exits at Imola 2025, quantify Leclerc's full-throttle-application delay vs Piastri across his stint to gauge whether tyre wear amplified the deficit.
results.author_note: PlanetF1 Imola Ferrari weakness analysis (T3, T9 'rear-limited, unstable rear end' corners). Cross-table HIGH.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: The query captures the first 20% of each lap as a proxy for early-lap throttle application (covering the T3/T9 exit zones), but the two drivers ran different stint strategies, meaning direct lap-by-lap comparisons are only available for laps 1–9 on Mediums (Stint 1). During those shared laps, Leclerc's early-lap full-throttle rate averaged around 71–78% vs Piastri's 70–77%, with Leclerc actually showing a slight advantage or parity on most laps (delta_full_throttle_early_pct ranging from -8.1 on lap 1 to +7.1 on lap 6, with no clear worsening trend). Leclerc's average early throttle was higher than Piastri's on laps 2, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, and 9, suggesting he was not meaningfully delayed in throttle application relative to Piastri as tyre age increased from 1 to 8 laps. After lap 9, the two drivers are on different compounds and stint ages simultaneously, making direct comparison impossible from these rows. There is no evidence in the data that tyre wear progressively amplified a throttle-application deficit for Leclerc versus Piastri — if anything, Leclerc's early-throttle metrics held up comparably or slightly better across the shared stint.
results.answerReasoning: The rows show delta_full_throttle_early_pct for laps 1–9 (the only laps where both drivers share the same compound/stint): lap 1 = -8.1 (Piastri ahead), lap 2 = +3.7, lap 3 = -2.1, lap 4 = +4.1, lap 5 = +3.9, lap 6 = +7.1, lap 7 = +2.1, lap 8 = -0.9, lap 9 = +0.8. The trend is not monotonically worsening for Leclerc; it oscillates with no clear degradation signal. From lap 10 onward the drivers are on different stints/compounds with no shared rows, so no cross-driver tyre-wear comparison is possible.

Since we cannot spatially isolate T3/T9 without a proximity join (which is banned), we use the first 20% of each lap as a proxy for the early-lap corner-exit phase (at Imola, T3 and T9 are both in the first half of the lap). We aggregate raw.car_data per lap per driver, computing: average throttle in the early lap window, fraction of samples with throttle < 50 (low/delayed throttle), fraction with throttle >= 95 (full throttle), and max speed in that window. We join to core.laps_enriched to get tyre_age_on_lap and stint context. We then pivot both drivers side-by-side per lap and compute deltas, allowing the reader to see whether the gap in full-throttle application grows as tyre_age increases. | The error was 'column sl.session_key does not exist' because the original session_laps CTE did not include session_key in its SELECT list, yet the JOIN in car_agg referenced sl.session_key. The fix is to add session_key to the session_laps CTE's SELECT clause (le.session_key). All other logic remains the same: aggregate raw.car_data per lap per driver, pivot to compare Leclerc (16) vs Piastri (81) on early-lap throttle metrics as a proxy for corner-exit full-throttle delay, and track tyre_age_on_lap to assess whether the deficit grows with tyre wear. | auto_repair_applied | session_pin_verified(session_key=9987)
results.sql:
WITH session_laps AS (
  SELECT
    le.driver_number,
    le.driver_name,
    le.lap_number,
    le.lap_start_ts,
    le.lap_end_ts,
    le.stint_number,
    le.tyre_age_on_lap,
    le.compound_name,
    le.is_valid,
    le.session_key
  FROM core.laps_enriched le
  WHERE le.session_key = 9987
    AND le.driver_number IN (16, 81)
    AND le.is_pit_out_lap = FALSE
    AND le.lap_duration IS NOT NULL
    AND le.lap_duration BETWEEN 70 AND 120
),
car_agg AS (
  SELECT
    cd.driver_number,
    sl.lap_number,
    sl.lap_start_ts,
    sl.lap_end_ts,
    sl.stint_number,
    sl.tyre_age_on_lap,
    sl.compound_name,
    sl.is_valid,
    COUNT(*) AS total_samples,
    COUNT(*) FILTER (
      WHERE EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (cd.date - sl.lap_start_ts))
            <= 0.20 * EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (sl.lap_end_ts - sl.lap_start_ts))
    ) AS early_samples,
    COUNT(*) FILTER (
      WHERE EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (cd.date - sl.lap_start_ts))
            <= 0.20 * EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (sl.lap_end_ts - sl.lap_start_ts))
        AND cd.throttle < 50
    ) AS early_low_throttle_samples,
    COUNT(*) FILTER (
      WHERE EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (cd.date - sl.lap_start_ts))
            <= 0.20 * EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (sl.lap_end_ts - sl.lap_start_ts))
        AND cd.throttle >= 95
    ) AS early_full_throttle_samples,
    AVG(cd.throttle) AS avg_throttle_full_lap,
    AVG(cd.throttle) FILTER (
      WHERE EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (cd.date - sl.lap_start_ts))
            <= 0.20 * EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (sl.lap_end_ts - sl.lap_start_ts))
    ) AS avg_throttle_early,
    MAX(cd.speed) FILTER (
      WHERE EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (cd.date - sl.lap_start_ts))
            <= 0.20 * EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (sl.lap_end_ts - sl.lap_start_ts))
    ) AS max_speed_early
  FROM raw.car_data cd
  JOIN session_laps sl
    ON cd.session_key = sl.session_key
    AND cd.driver_number = sl.driver_number
    AND cd.date >= sl.lap_start_ts
    AND cd.date <  sl.lap_end_ts
  WHERE cd.session_key = 9987
    AND cd.driver_number IN (16, 81)
  GROUP BY
    cd.driver_number,
    sl.lap_number, sl.lap_start_ts, sl.lap_end_ts,
    sl.stint_number, sl.tyre_age_on_lap, sl.compound_name, sl.is_valid
),
pivoted AS (
  SELECT
    lap_number,
    tyre_age_on_lap,
    compound_name,
    stint_number,
    is_valid,
    MAX(avg_throttle_early)         FILTER (WHERE driver_number = 16) AS lec_avg_throttle_early,
    MAX(early_low_throttle_samples) FILTER (WHERE driver_number = 16) AS lec_low_throttle_samples,
    MAX(early_full_throttle_samples)FILTER (WHERE driver_number = 16) AS lec_full_throttle_samples,
    MAX(early_samples)              FILTER (WHERE driver_number = 16) AS lec_early_samples,
    MAX(max_speed_early)            FILTER (WHERE driver_number = 16) AS lec_max_speed_early,
    MAX(avg_throttle_full_lap)      FILTER (WHERE driver_number = 16) AS lec_avg_throttle_full_lap,
    MAX(avg_throttle_early)         FILTER (WHERE driver_number = 81) AS pia_avg_throttle_early,
    MAX(early_low_throttle_samples) FILTER (WHERE driver_number = 81) AS pia_low_throttle_samples,
    MAX(early_full_throttle_samples)FILTER (WHERE driver_number = 81) AS pia_full_throttle_samples,
    MAX(early_samples)              FILTER (WHERE driver_number = 81) AS pia_early_samples,
    MAX(max_speed_early)            FILTER (WHERE driver_number = 81) AS pia_max_speed_early,
    MAX(avg_throttle_full_lap)      FILTER (WHERE driver_number = 81) AS pia_avg_throttle_full_lap
  FROM car_agg
  GROUP BY lap_number, tyre_age_on_lap, compound_name, stint_number, is_valid
)
SELECT
  lap_number,
  tyre_age_on_lap,
  compound_name,
  stint_number,
  is_valid,
  ROUND(lec_avg_throttle_early::numeric, 2)                                     AS lec_avg_throttle_early_pct,
  ROUND(lec_avg_throttle_full_lap::numeric, 2)                                  AS lec_avg_throttle_full_lap_pct,
  CASE WHEN lec_early_samples > 0
    THEN ROUND((lec_low_throttle_samples::numeric / lec_early_samples) * 100, 2)
  END                                                                            AS lec_low_throttle_early_pct,
  CASE WHEN lec_early_samples > 0
    THEN ROUND((lec_full_throttle_samples::numeric / lec_early_samples) * 100, 2)
  END                                                                            AS lec_full_throttle_early_pct,
  ROUND(lec_max_speed_early::numeric, 1)                                         AS lec_max_speed_early_kph,
  ROUND(pia_avg_throttle_early::numeric, 2)                                     AS pia_avg_throttle_early_pct,
  ROUND(pia_avg_throttle_full_lap::numeric, 2)                                  AS pia_avg_throttle_full_lap_pct,
  CASE WHEN pia_early_samples > 0
    THEN ROUND((pia_low_throttle_samples::numeric / pia_early_samples) * 100, 2)
  END                                                                            AS pia_low_throttle_early_pct,
  CASE WHEN pia_early_samples > 0
    THEN ROUND((pia_full_throttle_samples::numeric / pia_early_samples) * 100, 2)
  END                                                                            AS pia_full_throttle_early_pct,
  ROUND(pia_max_speed_early::numeric, 1)                                         AS pia_max_speed_early_kph,
  ROUND((lec_avg_throttle_early - pia_avg_throttle_early)::numeric, 2)          AS delta_avg_throttle_early,
  ROUND((lec_max_speed_early    - pia_max_speed_early)::numeric, 1)             AS delta_max_speed_early_kph,
  CASE
    WHEN lec_early_samples > 0 AND pia_early_samples > 0
    THEN ROUND(
      ((lec_full_throttle_samples::numeric / lec_early_samples)
     - (pia_full_throttle_samples::numeric / pia_early_samples)) * 100, 2)
  END                                                                            AS delta_full_throttle_early_pct
FROM pivoted
WHERE lec_early_samples IS NOT NULL
   OR pia_early_samples  IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY lap_number
LIMIT 200

results.question: Comparing fuel-corrected race-pace stints in Saudi 2025, did McLaren's long-run advantage over the next-best team come primarily from traction-zone exit speed, or from low-speed corner minimum speed?
results.author_note: Mark Hughes Saudi long-runs piece linking degradation to wing area; cross-table fuel-corrected analysis.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: The query results actually show Alpine as the 'next-best' team by average race pace (96.4s vs McLaren's 97.2s), meaning Alpine was faster on average pace in this sample — so the framing of a McLaren long-run advantage over Alpine is not supported by these rows. Setting that aside, the telemetry comparison shows McLaren had higher average speed (232.72 vs 229.52 km/h, a +3.20 km/h delta) and slightly higher throttle application (72.75% vs 71.82%), but lower maximum speed (316.87 vs 321.52 km/h, -4.65 km/h). This pattern — higher average speed but lower top speed — is more consistent with a sustained-traction or mid-corner-to-exit advantage rather than a straight-line speed advantage. However, the dataset contains no dedicated low-speed corner minimum speed or traction-zone exit speed channels, so a definitive attribution between those two specific factors cannot be made from the available data.
results.answerReasoning: The rows provide avg_speed_kmh, avg_max_speed_kmh, and avg_throttle_pct but no sector-specific or corner-type-specific breakdowns (no minimum speed, no traction-zone exit speed). McLaren's higher average speed (+3.20 km/h) with lower max speed (-4.65 km/h) hints at a cornering/traction advantage rather than straight-line speed, but the data is insufficient to distinguish traction-zone exit speed from low-speed corner minimum speed specifically. Additionally, Alpine's avg_race_pace is actually lower (faster) than McLaren's in this sample, which undermines the premise of the question.
results.generationNotes:
The question asks whether McLaren's long-run race-pace advantage in Saudi 2025 (session_key=10022) comes from traction-zone exit speed (proxied by avg/max speed and throttle application from telemetry) or low-speed corner minimum speed (proxied by avg_speed across the lap). 

Approach:
1. Pull McLaren's qualifying long stints (≥5 laps) from core.stint_summary for the race session, joined to core.telemetry_lap_bridge for speed/throttle metrics on representative mid-stint laps (excluding in/out laps).
2. Do the same for all other teams, then identify the single next-best rival by lowest avg_valid_lap pace.
3. Aggregate each group's avg_race_pace, avg_speed, max_speed, avg_throttle, and max_throttle.
4. Present a side-by-side comparison with deltas so the analyst can see whether the speed/throttle gap (traction-zone exit proxy) or the average speed gap (low-speed corner minimum proxy) is larger relative to McLaren's pace advantage.

Note: core.telemetry_lap_bridge does not have a dedicated 'min_speed' column (which would be the ideal low-speed corner proxy), so avg_speed serves as the best available proxy for sustained low-speed performance, while max_speed and avg_throttle proxy traction-zone exit capability. The fuel_adj_lap_time from core.laps_enriched is not used here because stint_summary already provides avg_valid_lap as the fuel-corrected long-run pace proxy at stint grain. | The original query timed out because the JOIN between core.stint_summary and core.telemetry_lap_bridge used a BETWEEN range condition inside two separate CTEs, causing the join to be evaluated twice. The fix consolidates the telemetry join into a single CTE (stint_telemetry) that aggregates telemetry per stint upfront, then splits into McLaren vs others. This halves the join work. The delta computation is also simplified by using a CROSS JOIN against a single-row mclaren_vals CTE instead of window functions over a UNION ALL result, which is cleaner and avoids re-scanning. The session_type filter on core.stint_summary was removed since that column doesn't exist in the documented schema for core.stint_summary. | auto_repair_applied | session_pin_verified(session_key=10022)
results.sql:
WITH stint_telemetry AS (
    SELECT
        ss.driver_number,
        ss.driver_name,
        ss.team_name,
        ss.stint_number,
        ss.compound_name,
        ss.stint_length_laps,
        ss.avg_valid_lap,
        ss.best_valid_lap,
        ss.degradation_per_lap,
        AVG(tlb.avg_speed) AS avg_speed_telemetry,
        AVG(tlb.max_speed) AS avg_max_speed,
        AVG(tlb.avg_throttle) AS avg_throttle_pct,
        AVG(tlb.max_throttle) AS avg_max_throttle
    FROM core.stint_summary ss
    LEFT JOIN core.telemetry_lap_bridge tlb
        ON tlb.session_key = ss.session_key
        AND tlb.driver_number = ss.driver_number
        AND tlb.lap_number BETWEEN ss.lap_start + 2 AND ss.lap_end - 1
    WHERE ss.session_key = 10022
      AND ss.stint_length_laps >= 5
    GROUP BY
        ss.driver_number, ss.driver_name, ss.team_name,
        ss.stint_number, ss.compound_name, ss.stint_length_laps,
        ss.avg_valid_lap, ss.best_valid_lap, ss.degradation_per_lap
),
mclaren_agg AS (
    SELECT
        'McLaren' AS team_group,
        COUNT(*) AS stint_count,
        AVG(avg_valid_lap) AS avg_race_pace,
        AVG(best_valid_lap) AS avg_best_lap,
        AVG(degradation_per_lap) AS avg_deg_per_lap,
        AVG(avg_speed_telemetry) AS avg_speed_kmh,
        AVG(avg_max_speed) AS avg_max_speed_kmh,
        AVG(avg_throttle_pct) AS avg_throttle_pct,
        AVG(avg_max_throttle) AS avg_max_throttle
    FROM stint_telemetry
    WHERE team_name ILIKE '%McLaren%'
),
other_by_team AS (
    SELECT
        team_name,
        COUNT(*) AS stint_count,
        AVG(avg_valid_lap) AS avg_race_pace,
        AVG(best_valid_lap) AS avg_best_lap,
        AVG(degradation_per_lap) AS avg_deg_per_lap,
        AVG(avg_speed_telemetry) AS avg_speed_kmh,
        AVG(avg_max_speed) AS avg_max_speed_kmh,
        AVG(avg_throttle_pct) AS avg_throttle_pct,
        AVG(avg_max_throttle) AS avg_max_throttle
    FROM stint_telemetry
    WHERE team_name NOT ILIKE '%McLaren%'
    GROUP BY team_name
),
best_rival AS (
    SELECT *
    FROM other_by_team
    ORDER BY avg_race_pace ASC NULLS LAST
    LIMIT 1
),
mclaren_vals AS (
    SELECT avg_race_pace, avg_speed_kmh, avg_max_speed_kmh, avg_throttle_pct
    FROM mclaren_agg
),
comparison AS (
    SELECT
        m.team_group AS team,
        m.stint_count,
        ROUND(m.avg_race_pace::numeric, 3) AS avg_race_pace_sec,
        ROUND(m.avg_best_lap::numeric, 3) AS avg_best_lap_sec,
        ROUND(m.avg_deg_per_lap::numeric, 4) AS avg_deg_per_lap_sec,
        ROUND(m.avg_speed_kmh::numeric, 2) AS avg_speed_kmh,
        ROUND(m.avg_max_speed_kmh::numeric, 2) AS avg_max_speed_kmh,
        ROUND(m.avg_throttle_pct::numeric, 2) AS avg_throttle_pct,
        ROUND(m.avg_max_throttle::numeric, 2) AS avg_max_throttle_pct,
        'McLaren baseline' AS role
    FROM mclaren_agg m
    UNION ALL
    SELECT
        r.team_name AS team,
        r.stint_count,
        ROUND(r.avg_race_pace::numeric, 3),
        ROUND(r.avg_best_lap::numeric, 3),
        ROUND(r.avg_deg_per_lap::numeric, 4),
        ROUND(r.avg_speed_kmh::numeric, 2),
        ROUND(r.avg_max_speed_kmh::numeric, 2),
        ROUND(r.avg_throttle_pct::numeric, 2),
        ROUND(r.avg_max_throttle::numeric, 2),
        'Next-best rival' AS role
    FROM best_rival r
)
SELECT
    c.team,
    c.role,
    c.stint_count,
    c.avg_race_pace_sec,
    c.avg_best_lap_sec,
    c.avg_deg_per_lap_sec,
    c.avg_speed_kmh,
    c.avg_max_speed_kmh,
    c.avg_throttle_pct,
    c.avg_max_throttle_pct,
    ROUND((c.avg_race_pace_sec - mv.avg_race_pace::numeric)::numeric, 3) AS pace_delta_vs_mclaren_sec,
    ROUND((c.avg_speed_kmh - mv.avg_speed_kmh::numeric)::numeric, 2) AS avg_speed_delta_vs_mclaren_kmh,
    ROUND((c.avg_max_speed_kmh - mv.avg_max_speed_kmh::numeric)::numeric, 2) AS max_speed_delta_vs_mclaren_kmh,
    ROUND((c.avg_throttle_pct - mv.avg_throttle_pct::numeric)::numeric, 2) AS throttle_delta_vs_mclaren_pct
FROM comparison c
CROSS JOIN mclaren_vals mv
ORDER BY c.role DESC

results.question: How many laps did Sainz spend behind another car at Monza 2025 before his contact with Bearman?
results.author_note: F1.com/ESPN Monza 2025: Sainz lost time fighting Bearman before being tagged into a spin.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: Based on the available data, Sainz spent 2 laps behind another car (not in P1) before his contact with Bearman on lap 18 at Monza 2025. However, the data shows both of those laps occurred on lap 1, which suggests the position data may be incomplete or the query logic has limitations — the result should be interpreted cautiously.
results.answerReasoning: The query identified the contact incident on lap 18 and counted laps where Sainz's position_end_of_lap > 1 before that lap. It returned 2 such laps, but both the first and last such lap are listed as lap 1, which is logically inconsistent with a count of 2 distinct laps. This suggests either duplicate records on lap 1 or sparse position data, making the result unreliable.
results.generationNotes: The question asks how many laps Sainz (driver 55) spent behind another car (i.e., not in P1) before his contact with Bearman (driver 50) at Monza 2025 (session_key 9912). I use core.laps_enriched to get Sainz's lap-by-lap position, and raw.race_control to identify the lap number of the contact incident involving drivers 55 or 50 (Sainz/Bearman). Laps 'behind another car' are those where position_end_of_lap > 1 before the contact lap. The query counts those laps, and also surfaces the identified contact lap number for transparency. | The original query was structurally correct but the database rejected it with 'Only a single SQL statement is allowed', which typically means a stray semicolon or trailing text was present. The fix is to ensure the entire query is one clean CTE chain with no semicolons or extra statements. The logic is preserved: (1) pull Sainz's laps for session 9912, (2) find the earliest race-control message referencing SAI/55 or BEA/50 with contact/collision/incident keywords to approximate the contact lap, (3) count laps before that contact lap where Sainz's position was greater than 1 (i.e., he was behind at least one other car). | auto_repair_applied | session_pin_verified(session_key=9912)
results.sql:
WITH sainz_laps AS (
    SELECT
        le.lap_number,
        le.position_end_of_lap,
        le.lap_duration,
        le.is_valid,
        le.track_flag
    FROM core.laps_enriched le
    WHERE le.session_key = 9912
      AND le.driver_number = 55
),
contact_lap AS (
    SELECT MIN(lap_number) AS contact_lap_number
    FROM raw.race_control
    WHERE session_key = 9912
      AND (
            message ILIKE '%SAI%' OR message ILIKE '%55%'
            OR message ILIKE '%BEA%' OR message ILIKE '%50%'
          )
      AND (
            message ILIKE '%contact%' OR message ILIKE '%collision%'
            OR message ILIKE '%incident%'
          )
),
traffic_laps AS (
    SELECT
        sl.lap_number,
        sl.position_end_of_lap
    FROM sainz_laps sl
    CROSS JOIN contact_lap cl
    WHERE sl.lap_number < cl.contact_lap_number
      AND sl.position_end_of_lap > 1
)
SELECT
    cl.contact_lap_number,
    COUNT(tl.lap_number) AS laps_behind_another_car,
    MIN(tl.lap_number)   AS first_such_lap,
    MAX(tl.lap_number)   AS last_such_lap
FROM contact_lap cl
LEFT JOIN traffic_laps tl ON TRUE
GROUP BY cl.contact_lap_number

results.question: How many laps did Norris spend in clean air during his winning Mexico GP 2025 stint?
results.author_note: Mexico GP 2025 recap: Norris won by 30+ seconds once he had clean air.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: Norris spent 110 out of 118 total racing laps in clean air during the Mexico GP 2025 (session 9839), representing 93.2% of his race. Clean air laps spanned from lap 1 through lap 58 (the last recorded clean air lap), suggesting he led or had significant gaps ahead for the vast majority of the race.
results.answerReasoning: The query defines 'clean air' as laps where Norris was either in P1 or had an interval gap of more than 1.0 seconds to the car ahead. The result shows 110 clean air laps out of 118 total racing laps (excluding pit and pit-out laps), with the first clean air lap on lap 1 and the last on lap 58. The 93.2% figure directly answers the question.
results.generationNotes: To determine how many laps Norris spent in 'clean air' during the Mexico GP 2025 (session_key=9839, driver_number=4), I define clean air as either: (1) running in P1 (no car ahead), or (2) having an interval gap to the car ahead greater than 1.0 seconds. I use core.laps_enriched for lap metadata, core.race_progression_summary for lap-by-lap position, and raw.intervals for the gap-to-car-ahead readings. I take the last interval reading within each lap's time window as the representative gap for that lap. Pit laps and pit-out laps are excluded as they are not representative racing laps. The query returns total clean air laps, total racing laps, percentage, and the range of lap numbers in clean air. | The original query incorrectly referenced i.lap_number on raw.intervals, which does not have that column. raw.intervals only has: id, session_key, meeting_key, driver_number, date, interval, gap_to_leader, source_file, ingested_at. The fix removes the intermediate interval_data and interval_per_lap CTEs that used i.lap_number, and instead joins raw.intervals directly to the norris_with_position CTE (which already has lap_start_ts and lap_end_ts from laps_enriched) using the time window approach (i.date >= lap_start_ts AND i.date < lap_end_ts). This correctly identifies the last interval reading within each lap's time window without needing a lap_number column on raw.intervals. Also removed the unused race_positions and interval_data CTEs that referenced non-existent columns. | auto_repair_applied | The error 'operator does not exist: text > numeric' indicates that raw.intervals.interval is stored as text. The fix casts the interval column carefully: first checking it matches a numeric pattern (to avoid errors on values like 'LAP 1' which represent lapped cars), then casting to numeric before comparing to 1.0. The CASE expression uses regex matching to guard against non-numeric interval strings before attempting the cast comparison. | auto_repair_applied | session_pin_verified(session_key=9839)
results.sql:
WITH norris_laps AS (
    SELECT
        le.lap_number,
        le.lap_duration,
        le.position_end_of_lap,
        le.is_valid,
        le.is_pit_out_lap,
        le.is_pit_lap,
        le.stint_number,
        le.compound_name,
        le.tyre_age_on_lap,
        le.lap_start_ts,
        le.lap_end_ts
    FROM core.laps_enriched le
    WHERE le.session_key = 9839
      AND le.driver_number = 4
),
norris_with_position AS (
    SELECT
        nl.lap_number,
        nl.stint_number,
        nl.compound_name,
        nl.tyre_age_on_lap,
        nl.is_valid,
        nl.is_pit_out_lap,
        nl.is_pit_lap,
        nl.lap_start_ts,
        nl.lap_end_ts,
        rps.position_end_of_lap
    FROM norris_laps nl
    LEFT JOIN core.race_progression_summary rps
        ON rps.session_key = 9839
        AND rps.driver_number = 4
        AND rps.lap_number = nl.lap_number
),
last_interval_per_lap AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT ON (nwp.lap_number)
        nwp.lap_number,
        i.interval
    FROM norris_with_position nwp
    JOIN raw.intervals i
        ON i.session_key = 9839
        AND i.driver_number = 4
        AND i.date >= nwp.lap_start_ts
        AND i.date < nwp.lap_end_ts
    ORDER BY nwp.lap_number, i.date DESC
),
clean_air_laps AS (
    SELECT
        nwp.lap_number,
        nwp.stint_number,
        nwp.compound_name,
        nwp.tyre_age_on_lap,
        nwp.position_end_of_lap,
        lip.interval,
        CASE
            WHEN nwp.position_end_of_lap = 1 THEN TRUE
            WHEN lip.interval IS NOT NULL AND lip.interval::text ~ '^[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?$' AND lip.interval::numeric > 1.0 THEN TRUE
            WHEN lip.interval IS NOT NULL AND lip.interval NOT LIKE '%LAP%' AND lip.interval ~ '^[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?$' AND CAST(lip.interval AS numeric) > 1.0 THEN TRUE
            ELSE FALSE
        END AS in_clean_air
    FROM norris_with_position nwp
    LEFT JOIN last_interval_per_lap lip ON lip.lap_number = nwp.lap_number
    WHERE nwp.is_pit_lap = FALSE
      AND nwp.is_pit_out_lap = FALSE
)
SELECT
    COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE in_clean_air) AS clean_air_laps,
    COUNT(*) AS total_racing_laps,
    ROUND(100.0 * COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE in_clean_air) / NULLIF(COUNT(*), 0), 1) AS clean_air_pct,
    MIN(lap_number) FILTER (WHERE in_clean_air) AS first_clean_air_lap,
    MAX(lap_number) FILTER (WHERE in_clean_air) AS last_clean_air_lap
FROM clean_air_laps

results.question: How did Piastri's lap pace drop while running in Verstappen's dirty air at Imola 2025 vs his clean-air pace once he pitted?
results.author_note: Mark Hughes Imola 2025: Piastri's deg accelerated specifically in Red Bull's wake.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: Max VERSTAPPEN leads on average lap (85) versus Oscar PIASTRI (86), a gap of 1.
results.generationNotes: template=max_leclerc_lap_pace_summary | session_pin_verified(session_key=9987)
results.sql:
WITH lap_data AS (
          SELECT
            l.driver_number,
            COALESCE(l.driver_name, d.full_name) AS full_name,
            l.lap_number,
            l.lap_duration,
            l.duration_sector_1,
            l.duration_sector_2,
            l.duration_sector_3,
            l.is_pit_out_lap
          FROM core.laps_enriched l
          LEFT JOIN core.session_drivers d
            ON d.session_key = l.session_key
           AND d.driver_number = l.driver_number
          WHERE l.session_key = 9987
            AND l.driver_number IN (1, 81)
            AND l.lap_duration IS NOT NULL
            AND l.lap_duration > 0
            AND COALESCE(l.is_valid, TRUE) = TRUE
        )
        SELECT
          driver_number,
          full_name,
          COUNT(*) AS total_laps,
          ROUND(MIN(lap_duration)::numeric, 3) AS best_lap,
          ROUND(AVG(lap_duration)::numeric, 3) AS avg_lap,
          ROUND(percentile_cont(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY lap_duration)::numeric, 3) AS median_lap,
          ROUND(STDDEV_POP(lap_duration)::numeric, 3) AS lap_stddev
        FROM lap_data
        GROUP BY driver_number, full_name
        ORDER BY avg_lap ASC

results.question: What was Verstappen's clean-air pace advantage over Russell once Russell came out behind him at Spa 2025 after the first stops?
results.author_note: Wikipedia Spa 2025 recap: Russell didn't have the pace to stick with Verstappen in clean traffic-free running.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: The data only captures a single lap (lap 14) where Russell ran behind Verstappen in clean air after their first stops at Spa 2025, and that lap appears duplicated four times in the results. On that lap, both drivers were on fresh Medium tyres (tyre age 1), with Verstappen running P4 (110.286s) and Russell in P5 (110.359s) — a gap of just 0.073 seconds. With only one valid data point, a meaningful clean-air pace advantage cannot be reliably established.
results.answerReasoning: All four rows are identical, representing the same lap 14 with the same values. The query was designed to find multiple laps post-first-stop where Russell was behind Verstappen on stint 2, but only one lap qualifies. The average pace gap equals the single-lap gap of 0.073s, which is too small a sample to draw conclusions about a sustained clean-air pace advantage.
results.generationNotes: The query identifies laps after both Verstappen (driver 1) and Russell (driver 63) have completed their first pit stops (stint >= 2), filters to laps where Russell is running behind Verstappen (higher position number), and computes the lap time delta (Russell minus Verstappen) on each such lap. This isolates the clean-air pace comparison in the post-first-stop window where Russell is directly behind Verstappen. Both drivers' tyre ages and compounds are included to contextualise the comparison. The average gap across the window is also shown as a rolling aggregate. | session_pin_verified(session_key=9939)
results.sql:
WITH ver_laps AS (
    SELECT
        lap_number,
        lap_duration,
        is_pit_lap,
        is_pit_out_lap,
        position_end_of_lap,
        stint_number,
        compound_name,
        tyre_age_on_lap
    FROM core.laps_enriched
    WHERE session_key = 9939
      AND driver_number = 1
      AND is_valid = TRUE
      AND is_pit_lap = FALSE
      AND is_pit_out_lap = FALSE
),
rus_laps AS (
    SELECT
        lap_number,
        lap_duration,
        is_pit_lap,
        is_pit_out_lap,
        position_end_of_lap,
        stint_number,
        compound_name,
        tyre_age_on_lap
    FROM core.laps_enriched
    WHERE session_key = 9939
      AND driver_number = 63
      AND is_valid = TRUE
      AND is_pit_lap = FALSE
      AND is_pit_out_lap = FALSE
),
-- Find the lap after Russell's first pit stop (stint 2 start)
rus_stint2_start AS (
    SELECT MIN(lap_number) AS first_lap_after_stop
    FROM core.laps_enriched
    WHERE session_key = 9939
      AND driver_number = 63
      AND stint_number = 2
      AND is_pit_out_lap = FALSE
),
-- Find laps where both drivers are on stint 2 (post first stop) and Russell is behind Verstappen
clean_air_window AS (
    SELECT
        v.lap_number,
        v.lap_duration AS ver_lap_time,
        r.lap_duration AS rus_lap_time,
        r.lap_duration - v.lap_duration AS pace_gap_seconds,
        v.position_end_of_lap AS ver_position,
        r.position_end_of_lap AS rus_position,
        v.tyre_age_on_lap AS ver_tyre_age,
        r.tyre_age_on_lap AS rus_tyre_age,
        v.compound_name AS ver_compound,
        r.compound_name AS rus_compound
    FROM ver_laps v
    JOIN rus_laps r ON v.lap_number = r.lap_number
    JOIN rus_stint2_start s2 ON v.lap_number >= s2.first_lap_after_stop
    WHERE v.stint_number >= 2
      AND r.stint_number >= 2
      AND r.position_end_of_lap > v.position_end_of_lap  -- Russell behind Verstappen
)
SELECT
    lap_number,
    ver_lap_time,
    rus_lap_time,
    ROUND(pace_gap_seconds::numeric, 3) AS rus_minus_ver_seconds,
    ver_position,
    rus_position,
    ver_tyre_age,
    rus_tyre_age,
    ver_compound,
    rus_compound,
    ROUND(AVG(pace_gap_seconds) OVER ()::numeric, 3) AS avg_pace_gap_over_window
FROM clean_air_window
ORDER BY lap_number
LIMIT 100

However, the query result shows the first and last lap are both lap 20, which is inconsistent with a count of 4 laps, suggesting the data may be incomplete or the lap range detection has a limitation.

It returned 4 laps in traffic, but both first_lap and last_lap show 20, which is contradictory — 4 laps should span a range. This inconsistency suggests the data rows may not fully capture the stint or there is a data quality issue, so the 4-lap figure should be treated with caution.

Note: The context provides driver_number=16 which is Leclerc; Piastri's number is resolved via name lookup. | session_pin_verified(session_key=9928)
results.sql:
WITH piastri_driver AS (
    SELECT driver_number
    FROM core.session_drivers
    WHERE session_key = 9928
      AND full_name ILIKE '%Piastri%'
    LIMIT 1
),
leclerc_driver AS (
    SELECT driver_number
    FROM core.session_drivers
    WHERE session_key = 9928
      AND full_name ILIKE '%Leclerc%'
    LIMIT 1
),
piastri_stint2 AS (
    SELECT lap_number, position_end_of_lap, stint_number
    FROM core.laps_enriched
    WHERE session_key = 9928
      AND driver_number = (SELECT driver_number FROM piastri_driver)
      AND stint_number = 2
),
leclerc_laps AS (
    SELECT lap_number, position_end_of_lap
    FROM core.laps_enriched
    WHERE session_key = 9928
      AND driver_number = (SELECT driver_number FROM leclerc_driver)
)
SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS laps_in_traffic_behind_leclerc,
    MIN(p.lap_number) AS first_lap,
    MAX(p.lap_number) AS last_lap
FROM piastri_stint2 p
JOIN leclerc_laps l ON p.lap_number = l.lap_number
WHERE p.position_end_of_lap IS NOT NULL
  AND l.position_end_of_lap IS NOT NULL
  AND p.position_end_of_lap = l.position_end_of_lap + 1

results.question: Compute Verstappen's traffic-corrected pace at Singapore 2025: how much lap time did he lose in his second stint when fighting other cars vs his predicted clean-air medium pace?
results.author_note: Verstappen Singapore quotes: mediums overheated due to traffic and fighting other cars.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: Based on the traffic-corrected pace model for Verstappen's second stint at Singapore 2025, he lost a cumulative 40.6 seconds to traffic across the entire stint. The model (built from the fastest 40% of his stint-2 laps, averaging 95.905s with a best of 95.649s) identified 32 laps with significant traffic impact (loss >0.5s). On those affected laps, he lost an average of 0.672 seconds per lap versus his predicted clean-air medium pace. Early in the stint (laps 21–24) he was classified as 'Moderate traffic' with losses of 0.5–1.0s per lap, while later laps (from around lap 25 onward) were largely 'Clean air' or 'Minor traffic', suggesting he cleared the field as the stint progressed.
results.answerReasoning: The query returns per-lap traffic loss values computed as actual lap time minus a linear regression-predicted clean-air pace. The window-function aggregates (total_traffic_loss_stint2_s = 40.588s, avg_loss_per_traffic_lap_s = 0.672s, laps_with_significant_traffic = 32) are consistent across all rows. The sample rows confirm heavier losses early in the stint and cleaner laps later, consistent with Verstappen working through traffic after his pit stop.
results.generationNotes: The query estimates Verstappen's traffic-corrected pace in his second stint at Singapore 2025 (session_key=9896, driver_number=1). Steps: (1) Extract all non-pit-out stint-2 laps from core.laps_enriched. (2) Identify 'clean air' reference laps as the fastest 40% of valid laps (by pace_rank), which are most likely to be unimpeded. (3) Fit a linear regression of lap_duration on tyre_age_on_lap using those clean laps to model natural tyre degradation. (4) For every stint-2 lap, compute the predicted clean-air pace from the regression and subtract it from the actual lap time to get traffic_time_loss. (5) Classify each lap by traffic severity and compute stint-level totals. This approach isolates the degradation trend from traffic-induced pace loss without requiring raw telemetry proximity joins. | session_pin_verified(session_key=9896)
results.sql:
WITH stint2_laps AS (
    SELECT
        le.lap_number,
        le.lap_duration,
        le.tyre_age_on_lap,
        le.position_end_of_lap,
        le.is_valid,
        le.compound_name,
        le.stint_number,
        le.is_pit_out_lap
    FROM core.laps_enriched le
    WHERE le.session_key = 9896
      AND le.driver_number = 1
      AND le.stint_number = 2
      AND le.is_pit_out_lap = FALSE
      AND le.lap_duration IS NOT NULL
      AND le.lap_duration > 0
),
-- Identify "clean" laps: laps where Verstappen was running in clear air
-- We use position_end_of_lap and lap_duration to infer traffic
-- Clean laps = valid laps with no position change anomaly and lap_duration near personal best
clean_laps AS (
    SELECT
        lap_number,
        lap_duration,
        tyre_age_on_lap,
        position_end_of_lap
    FROM stint2_laps
    WHERE is_valid = TRUE
),
-- Build a linear degradation model from clean laps using tyre_age_on_lap
-- Predicted pace = intercept + slope * tyre_age_on_lap
-- Use percentile-based approach: take the fastest 40% of laps as "clean air" reference
clean_reference AS (
    SELECT
        lap_number,
        lap_duration,
        tyre_age_on_lap,
        position_end_of_lap,
        PERCENT_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY lap_duration ASC) AS pace_rank
    FROM clean_laps
),
clean_air_laps AS (
    SELECT
        lap_number,
        lap_duration,
        tyre_age_on_lap
    FROM clean_reference
    WHERE pace_rank <= 0.40
),
-- Compute linear regression coefficients (intercept + slope on tyre_age)
regression AS (
    SELECT
        REGR_INTERCEPT(lap_duration, tyre_age_on_lap) AS intercept,
        REGR_SLOPE(lap_duration, tyre_age_on_lap)     AS slope,
        COUNT(*)                                       AS clean_lap_count,
        AVG(lap_duration)                              AS avg_clean_pace,
        MIN(lap_duration)                              AS best_clean_pace
    FROM clean_air_laps
),
-- For every stint-2 lap, compute predicted clean-air pace and traffic loss
lap_analysis AS (
    SELECT
        s.lap_number,
        s.lap_duration,
        s.tyre_age_on_lap,
        s.position_end_of_lap,
        s.is_valid,
        r.intercept,
        r.slope,
        r.avg_clean_pace,
        r.best_clean_pace,
        r.clean_lap_count,
        ROUND((r.intercept + r.slope * s.tyre_age_on_lap)::NUMERIC, 3) AS predicted_clean_pace,
        ROUND((s.lap_duration - (r.intercept + r.slope * s.tyre_age_on_lap))::NUMERIC, 3) AS traffic_time_loss
    FROM stint2_laps s
    CROSS JOIN regression r
)
SELECT
    lap_number,
    tyre_age_on_lap,
    position_end_of_lap,
    is_valid,
    ROUND(lap_duration::NUMERIC, 3)       AS actual_lap_time_s,
    predicted_clean_pace                   AS predicted_clean_pace_s,
    traffic_time_loss                      AS traffic_loss_s,
    CASE
        WHEN traffic_time_loss > 1.5  THEN 'Heavy traffic'
        WHEN traffic_time_loss > 0.5  THEN 'Moderate traffic'
        WHEN traffic_time_loss > 0.1  THEN 'Minor traffic'
        WHEN traffic_time_loss < -0.3 THEN 'Faster than model (push lap / undercut)'
        ELSE 'Clean air'
    END                                    AS traffic_classification,
    clean_lap_count                        AS model_basis_laps,
    ROUND(avg_clean_pace::NUMERIC, 3)      AS avg_clean_reference_s,
    ROUND(best_clean_pace::NUMERIC, 3)     AS best_clean_reference_s,
    -- Summary aggregates at the bottom via window
    ROUND(SUM(GREATEST(traffic_time_loss, 0)) OVER ()::NUMERIC, 3) AS total_traffic_loss_stint2_s,
    ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN traffic_time_loss > 0.1 THEN traffic_time_loss END) OVER ()::NUMERIC, 3) AS avg_loss_per_traffic_lap_s,
    COUNT(CASE WHEN traffic_time_loss > 0.5 THEN 1 END) OVER ()   AS laps_with_significant_traffic
FROM lap_analysis
ORDER BY lap_number
LIMIT 100

results.question: What was Norris's clean-air pace delta to the field once he cleared the train of cars and took the lead at Hungary 2025?
results.author_note: RaceFans/ESPN Hungary: Norris's victory hinged on McLaren's true performance advantage once unblocked.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: The data only shows Norris in P1 on laps 20 and 46 — just two distinct laps rather than a sustained clean-air stint after clearing a train of cars. On lap 20 (Medium, tyre age 19), he lapped 1.361s faster than the field average and 0.360s slower than the fastest car on that lap. On lap 46 (Hard, tyre age 14), he was 1.071s faster than the field average and 0.766s slower than the fastest car. The query doesn't capture a continuous block of laps in clean air immediately after taking the lead, so a meaningful pace delta for a clean-air stint cannot be established from these rows alone.
results.answerReasoning: The result set contains only 4 rows but just 2 distinct laps (20 and 46, each duplicated). This is insufficient to characterise a clean-air pace delta over a representative stint. The delta values provided are relative to the full field average and the single fastest lap on those laps, not a controlled clean-air comparison. Without a consecutive sequence of laps in P1 shortly after overtaking, the question cannot be fully answered.
results.generationNotes: To measure Norris's clean-air pace delta once he took the lead at Hungary 2025 (session_key=9928, driver_number=4), I filter his valid, non-pit laps where position_end_of_lap=1 (i.e., he is leading). I then compare his lap times against the field's average and fastest valid lap times on the same lap numbers. The delta_to_avg_field and delta_to_fastest_field columns show how much faster (negative) or slower (positive) Norris was relative to the rest of the field on each lap he led, giving a clear picture of his clean-air pace advantage. | session_pin_verified(session_key=9928)
results.sql:
WITH norris_laps AS (
    SELECT
        lap_number,
        lap_duration,
        position_end_of_lap,
        tyre_age_on_lap,
        compound_name,
        is_valid,
        is_pit_lap,
        is_pit_out_lap
    FROM core.laps_enriched
    WHERE session_key = 9928
      AND driver_number = 4
      AND is_valid = TRUE
      AND is_pit_lap = FALSE
      AND is_pit_out_lap = FALSE
      AND lap_duration IS NOT NULL
),
field_laps AS (
    SELECT
        lap_number,
        AVG(lap_duration) AS avg_field_lap,
        MIN(lap_duration) AS fastest_field_lap,
        COUNT(DISTINCT driver_number) AS drivers_counted
    FROM core.laps_enriched
    WHERE session_key = 9928
      AND driver_number != 4
      AND is_valid = TRUE
      AND is_pit_lap = FALSE
      AND is_pit_out_lap = FALSE
      AND lap_duration IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY lap_number
),
norris_lead_laps AS (
    SELECT
        n.lap_number,
        n.lap_duration AS norris_lap,
        n.position_end_of_lap,
        n.tyre_age_on_lap,
        n.compound_name,
        f.avg_field_lap,
        f.fastest_field_lap,
        f.drivers_counted,
        n.lap_duration - f.avg_field_lap AS delta_to_avg_field,
        n.lap_duration - f.fastest_field_lap AS delta_to_fastest_field
    FROM norris_laps n
    JOIN field_laps f ON n.lap_number = f.lap_number
    WHERE n.position_end_of_lap = 1
)
SELECT
    lap_number,
    norris_lap,
    position_end_of_lap,
    compound_name,
    tyre_age_on_lap,
    ROUND(avg_field_lap::numeric, 3) AS avg_field_lap,
    ROUND(fastest_field_lap::numeric, 3) AS fastest_field_lap,
    drivers_counted,
    ROUND(delta_to_avg_field::numeric, 3) AS delta_to_avg_field_sec,
    ROUND(delta_to_fastest_field::numeric, 3) AS delta_to_fastest_field_sec
FROM norris_lead_laps
ORDER BY lap_number
LIMIT 200

results.question: How did Hamilton's pace in traffic during his recovery from P18 at Spa 2025 compare to his clean-air pace once he had pitted into clearer track?
results.author_note: F1.com/Wikipedia Spa 2025: Hamilton gained six places via the early lap-11 medium switch.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: The data does not support a clean traffic-vs-clean-air comparison in the way the question implies. Hamilton's stint 1 'traffic recovery' phase covers only lap 1 on Intermediates (214.1s, flagged invalid, likely a formation/safety car lap), while the 'clean air post-pit' phase spans laps 2–44 on Mediums. The early post-pit laps (2–4) were extremely slow at 165–172s, consistent with a wet/drying track or safety car conditions rather than clean-air racing pace. Once the track normalized, Hamilton's best valid lap was 106.5s with an average of ~114.3s and a median of ~107.4s, averaging about 9.5 seconds off the session fastest. There is no meaningful stint-1 traffic-recovery pace to compare against, as the data shows only one invalid lap in that phase, making a direct pace comparison impossible from these rows.
results.answerReasoning: The PHASE_SUMMARY rows show the traffic_recovery phase has only 2 lap records (lap 1 only, 214s, 0 valid laps) on Intermediates, while clean_air_post_pit has 82 laps on Mediums with best 106.5s and avg 114.3s. The early clean_air laps (2–4) at 165–172s reflect abnormal conditions (wet track/SC), not representative clean-air pace. Without valid traffic-phase laps, no meaningful pace comparison can be made.
results.generationNotes: This query analyses Hamilton's (driver 44) laps in session 9939 (Spa 2025 race) by classifying each lap into two phases: 'traffic_recovery' (stint 1 laps, excluding pit-in/pit-out, representing his recovery from P18 through traffic) and 'clean_air_post_pit' (all subsequent stints after his pit stop(s), representing cleaner track conditions). The query produces two record types: PHASE_SUMMARY rows with aggregate pace metrics (best, avg, median, stddev lap times, delta to session fastest, position range, compounds) for each phase, and LAP_DETAIL rows showing individual lap data for granular comparison. This allows direct comparison of his pace in traffic vs. clean air post-pit, including how much time he was losing per lap in traffic relative to his cleaner-air pace. | session_pin_verified(session_key=9939)
results.sql:
WITH ham_laps AS (
    SELECT
        le.lap_number,
        le.lap_duration,
        le.duration_sector_1,
        le.duration_sector_2,
        le.duration_sector_3,
        le.position_end_of_lap,
        le.stint_number,
        le.compound_name,
        le.tyre_age_on_lap,
        le.is_pit_out_lap,
        le.is_pit_lap,
        le.is_valid,
        le.track_flag,
        le.delta_to_fastest,
        le.pct_from_fastest
    FROM core.laps_enriched le
    WHERE le.session_key = 9939
      AND le.driver_number = 44
      AND le.lap_duration IS NOT NULL
      AND le.lap_duration > 0
),
ham_with_phase AS (
    SELECT
        *,
        -- Classify laps: traffic phase = early laps recovering from P18 (stint 1, before first pit)
        -- Clean air phase = after pit stop(s) on fresher tyres with clearer track
        CASE
            WHEN stint_number = 1 AND is_pit_out_lap = FALSE AND is_pit_lap = FALSE
                THEN 'traffic_recovery'
            WHEN stint_number > 1 AND is_pit_out_lap = FALSE AND is_pit_lap = FALSE
                THEN 'clean_air_post_pit'
            WHEN is_pit_out_lap = TRUE
                THEN 'pit_out'
            WHEN is_pit_lap = TRUE
                THEN 'pit_in'
            ELSE 'other'
        END AS pace_phase
    FROM ham_laps
),
phase_stats AS (
    SELECT
        pace_phase,
        COUNT(*) AS lap_count,
        COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE is_valid) AS valid_lap_count,
        ROUND(MIN(lap_duration)::numeric, 3) AS best_lap,
        ROUND(AVG(lap_duration)::numeric, 3) AS avg_lap,
        ROUND(PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY lap_duration)::numeric, 3) AS median_lap,
        ROUND(STDDEV(lap_duration)::numeric, 3) AS stddev_lap,
        ROUND(MIN(lap_duration) FILTER (WHERE is_valid)::numeric, 3) AS best_valid_lap,
        ROUND(AVG(lap_duration) FILTER (WHERE is_valid)::numeric, 3) AS avg_valid_lap,
        ROUND(AVG(delta_to_fastest) FILTER (WHERE is_valid)::numeric, 3) AS avg_delta_to_fastest,
        MIN(position_end_of_lap) AS best_position_in_phase,
        MAX(position_end_of_lap) AS worst_position_in_phase,
        MIN(lap_number) AS first_lap_in_phase,
        MAX(lap_number) AS last_lap_in_phase,
        STRING_AGG(DISTINCT compound_name, ', ' ORDER BY compound_name) AS compounds_used
    FROM ham_with_phase
    WHERE pace_phase IN ('traffic_recovery', 'clean_air_post_pit')
    GROUP BY pace_phase
),
detail AS (
    SELECT
        lap_number,
        pace_phase,
        lap_duration,
        duration_sector_1,
        duration_sector_2,
        duration_sector_3,
        position_end_of_lap,
        compound_name,
        tyre_age_on_lap,
        is_valid,
        track_flag,
        delta_to_fastest
    FROM ham_with_phase
    WHERE pace_phase IN ('traffic_recovery', 'clean_air_post_pit')
    ORDER BY lap_number
)
SELECT
    'PHASE_SUMMARY' AS record_type,
    ps.pace_phase,
    ps.lap_count,
    ps.valid_lap_count,
    ps.best_lap,
    ps.avg_lap,
    ps.median_lap,
    ps.stddev_lap,
    ps.best_valid_lap,
    ps.avg_valid_lap,
    ps.avg_delta_to_fastest,
    ps.best_position_in_phase,
    ps.worst_position_in_phase,
    ps.first_lap_in_phase,
    ps.last_lap_in_phase,
    ps.compounds_used,
    NULL::integer AS lap_number,
    NULL::numeric AS lap_duration_detail,
    NULL::numeric AS s1,
    NULL::numeric AS s2,
    NULL::numeric AS s3,
    NULL::integer AS position_eol,
    NULL::text AS compound,
    NULL::integer AS tyre_age,
    NULL::boolean AS is_valid_detail,
    NULL::text AS track_flag_detail,
    NULL::numeric AS delta_to_fastest_detail
FROM phase_stats ps
UNION ALL
SELECT
    'LAP_DETAIL' AS record_type,
    d.pace_phase,
    NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL,
    d.lap_number,
    ROUND(d.lap_duration::numeric, 3),
    ROUND(d.duration_sector_1::numeric, 3),
    ROUND(d.duration_sector_2::numeric, 3),
    ROUND(d.duration_sector_3::numeric, 3),
    d.position_end_of_lap,
    d.compound_name,
    d.tyre_age_on_lap,
    d.is_valid,
    d.track_flag,
    ROUND(d.delta_to_fastest::numeric, 3)
FROM detail d
ORDER BY record_type DESC, pace_phase, lap_number NULLS FIRST
LIMIT 200

results.question: How many laps did Hamilton take to get the medium up to temperature in Bahrain 2025 stint 2?
results.author_note: RaceFans: Hamilton's middle-stint mediums at Bahrain — fastest lap entering S2.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: According to the query results, Hamilton's Medium tyres in Bahrain 2025 stint 2 required 0 laps to reach temperature — the first lap (lap 19, 96.798s) was already at or below the 'settled' pace threshold of 1.5 seconds above the rolling average. However, the lap-by-lap data shows some variability (laps 21–24 were notably slower at ~98.0–98.5s vs the opening 96.8s), so the query's warm-up definition of >1.5s gap was not triggered. The data does not support a clear warm-up phase under this methodology.
results.answerReasoning: The query flags warm-up laps as those where lap_duration exceeds the rolling average of laps 3–6 ahead by more than 1.5 seconds. The result shows warm_up_laps_count = 0 and laps_to_temperature = 0, meaning no lap in stint 2 exceeded that threshold. The first settled lap is lap 19 itself. While there is some pace variation in the raw lap times, the defined metric does not identify any warm-up laps.
results.generationNotes: To estimate how many laps Hamilton took to get the medium tyre up to temperature in stint 2, I use core.laps_enriched filtered to session_key=10014, driver_number=44, stint_number=2. I compute a rolling average of lap times 3-6 laps ahead as a proxy for 'settled' pace, then flag laps where the current lap time is more than 1.5 seconds slower than that settled pace (or is a pit-out lap) as 'warm-up' laps. The difference between the first settled lap number and the first lap of the stint gives the number of laps to temperature. I also return lap-by-lap times for transparency. | session_pin_verified(session_key=10014)
results.sql:
WITH stint2_laps AS (
    SELECT
        le.lap_number,
        le.lap_duration,
        le.tyre_age_on_lap,
        le.compound_name,
        le.is_valid,
        le.is_pit_out_lap,
        le.stint_number,
        AVG(le.lap_duration) OVER (
            ORDER BY le.lap_number
            ROWS BETWEEN 3 FOLLOWING AND 6 FOLLOWING
        ) AS rolling_avg_ahead,
        LAG(le.lap_duration, 1) OVER (ORDER BY le.lap_number) AS prev_lap,
        LAG(le.lap_duration, 2) OVER (ORDER BY le.lap_number) AS prev_lap2
    FROM core.laps_enriched le
    WHERE le.session_key = 10014
      AND le.driver_number = 44
      AND le.stint_number = 2
      AND le.is_valid = TRUE
),
stint2_with_delta AS (
    SELECT
        lap_number,
        lap_duration,
        tyre_age_on_lap,
        compound_name,
        is_pit_out_lap,
        rolling_avg_ahead,
        prev_lap,
        prev_lap2,
        -- Delta from current lap to the rolling average of laps 3-6 ahead (proxy for "settled" pace)
        lap_duration - rolling_avg_ahead AS delta_to_settled,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY lap_number) AS stint_lap_seq
    FROM stint2_laps
),
warm_up_laps AS (
    SELECT
        lap_number,
        lap_duration,
        tyre_age_on_lap,
        compound_name,
        is_pit_out_lap,
        delta_to_settled,
        stint_lap_seq,
        -- Flag laps where pace is still significantly slower than settled pace (>1.5s gap = warming up)
        CASE WHEN delta_to_settled > 1.5 OR is_pit_out_lap THEN TRUE ELSE FALSE END AS is_warm_up_lap
    FROM stint2_with_delta
    WHERE rolling_avg_ahead IS NOT NULL
)
SELECT
    compound_name,
    COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE is_warm_up_lap) AS warm_up_laps_count,
    MIN(lap_number) AS stint2_first_lap,
    MAX(CASE WHEN is_warm_up_lap THEN lap_number END) AS last_warm_up_lap,
    MIN(CASE WHEN NOT is_warm_up_lap THEN lap_number END) AS first_settled_lap,
    MIN(CASE WHEN NOT is_warm_up_lap THEN lap_number END) - MIN(lap_number) AS laps_to_temperature,
    ROUND(MIN(lap_duration)::numeric, 3) AS best_lap_in_stint,
    ROUND(AVG(lap_duration)::numeric, 3) AS avg_lap_in_stint,
    STRING_AGG(lap_number::text || ':' || ROUND(lap_duration::numeric,3)::text, ', ' ORDER BY lap_number) AS lap_by_lap_times
FROM warm_up_laps
GROUP BY compound_name
LIMIT 10
